## Overview
Danfei Xu is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Interactive Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of **robot learning** and **computer vision**. His work develops integrated perception–action systems that enable robots to reason about 3D structure, object pose, and long-horizon manipulation from multi-modal data. Danfei’s research emphasizes <u>generalization across embodiments and tasks</u>, building scalable datasets and models for imitation and planning, and fusing visual, depth, and tactile sensing to support robust manipulation in real-world environments. His portfolio spans foundational contributions in **3D reconstruction**, **6D pose estimation**, and **learning from demonstrations**, as well as applied systems that leverage large datasets and language models for robot task planning. His work is highly cited (total citations: **15,157**) and bridges theory, datasets, and deployed robotic capabilities.

---

## Research Areas
Danfei Xu’s research sits at the confluence of *robot learning* and *computer vision*, with detailed contributions across several interlocking areas. He has advanced methods for <u>3D scene and object reconstruction</u> (e.g., learning-based single- and multi-view reconstruction) and dense multi-sensor fusion for accurate 6D pose and 3D bounding estimation, as exemplified by work on **3D-R2N2** and **DenseFusion**. In robot learning and imitation, he studies how to scale learning from human demonstrations and play data to achieve long-horizon manipulation — developing methods for *one-shot* and long-horizon generalization (Neural Task Programming, Neural Task Graphs, and subsequent imitation-learning studies). Danfei also focuses on building and curating large, diverse datasets and models to improve cross-embodiment transfer and generalization (e.g., Open X-Embodiment, EgoMimic), and on integrating language and large models for situated task planning (ProgPrompt / Progprompt). Additional strands include tactile perception for contact-rich manipulation, optimization- and learning-based task & motion planning, and generative planning techniques (diffusion-based skill chaining) for long-horizon tasks. Across these topics he repeatedly addresses the core problems of representation learning, sensor fusion, and dataset-driven generalization for robot autonomy.

---

## Notable Works
- "3D-R2N2: A unified approach for single and multi-view 3D object reconstruction" (2016) — introduced a recurrent neural architecture that learns shape priors to reconstruct 3D objects from single or multiple images, helping to establish learning-based 3D reconstruction as a practical approach for downstream robotic perception tasks.
- "DenseFusion: 6D object pose estimation by iterative dense fusion" (2019) — proposed an iterative dense fusion of RGB and depth features for robust, real-time 6D pose estimation in cluttered scenes, substantially improving pose accuracy by tightly integrating image and point-cloud information.
- "Open X-Embodiment: Robotic learning datasets and RT-X models" (2023) — curated a large, multi-institutional dataset and advocated for RT-X models that enable cross-robot generalization, advancing the community’s ability to train high-capacity models that transfer across diverse robot embodiments and environments.

---

## Academic Background
Danfei Xu’s publication record spans top venues in robotics and computer vision (CVPR, ICRA, NeurIPS and related conferences), reflecting a career trajectory from early work on tactile exploration and deformable-object manipulation to leading contributions in 3D perception, pose estimation, imitation learning, and dataset-driven robot learning. Over the past decade he has collaborated broadly with research groups across computer vision, robotics, and autonomous driving, producing influential papers on sensor fusion (PointFusion), scene understanding (scene graph generation), and scalable imitation strategies (MimicPlay, EgoMimic). As an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech’s School of Interactive Computing he maintains an active lab, contributes to open datasets and tools, and continues to publish high-impact work that shapes both theoretical methods and practical robot systems. For more information and publications, see his homepage: https://faculty.cc.gatech.edu/~danfei/.
